But while I was doing that, I had met Brenda, my second wife, at the Lewiston Police Department. That's where I got to know her. and then later on I hired her to. Be our chief investigator for my company. And we had a company of about 81 people, uh, pretty good size. We worked in several different states and, uh, all types of investigations, uh, for the most notable ones was the first Forest Service officer that was killed in the line of duty. Brett Jacobsen, uh, investigated that case, and that's a whole nother story. But killed by a gang of men called the Pratt Brothers. And, uh, he got caught, was sent to Lompoc Prison in California. He served several years there, escaped and got out and came to northern Idaho, where he met the frat brothers. And they started a bank robbery gang. They traveled all over various states in the northwest, robbing banks. Well, as time went along, Christopher Boyce was recaptured. But the Pratt brothers were not, uh. They didn't even know who they were, I don't think, at the time. But the Pratt brothers continued their, uh, Robbery, but not with banks. They changed. Switched their method of operation and started rubbing drug dealers. And so they would go to make home invasions. Yeah. And in northern Idaho, they made a home invasion. Turned bad on them. Police were called and a shootout ensued. And in the pursuit of Brett Jacobson, the Forest Service officer joined them and they were trailing him through the snow covered mountains and were caught in an ambush. And he was killed. And they finally caught all the Pratt brothers. One of them was wounded. They went to jail. That's making a long story short. Yeah. I'm sure. Yeah, that's very true. Well, after all that excitement, it is kind of funny that people will come back to a more quiet place. Because you bought the store in Harpster, right? Yep. After we worked the Gem State business until 2003 2003, and then Brenda and I bought the Harvester store and RV park, and we run that for 23 years. I think that's. Quite a while. A while, yeah. Yeah. And you're truly retired now. Truly retired. Brenda still works at H&R block. Yeah, but I'm retired. Yeah. Well, it sounds like probably you needed to be retired, huh? Yeah. I asked her if she could find another flight that got in earlier, which she did. She found one that got in at 10:00 in Spokane instead of midnight, which is two hours, which makes some difference. Um, the second chapter, I guess would be high school years. Um, when I first noticed girls, uh, well, I noticed girls before that, but when I really started getting serious about it and I started going with a girl by Sharon Shepherd first for maybe a year. lines.line: # Early Life and Family Background

> The speaker describes their birth in Grangeville and upbringing in Harpster and McComas Meadows. They detail their family lineage, including their grandfather Lloyd Ruby and great uncle Jess McComas, and recount the family's settlement history in the area. The narrative includes the movement of the Shear family into Happy Hollow in 1877 and the speaker's childhood spent in Harpster until moving to Grangeville in 1954. The family's connection to the land, including the Ferris and Whitman homesteads, is emphasized, highlighting the generational ties and sense of home.

metadata.title: Early Life and Family Background
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes their birth in Grangeville and upbringing in Harpster and McComas Meadows. They detail their family lineage, including their grandfather Lloyd Ruby and great uncle Jess McComas, and recount the family's settlement history in the area. The narrative includes the movement of the Shear family into Happy Hollow in 1877 and the speaker's childhood spent in Harpster until moving to Grangeville in 1954. The family's connection to the land, including the Ferris and Whitman homesteads, is emphasized, highlighting the generational ties and sense of home.

lines.line: # Connection to McComas Meadows and Camp 58

> The conversation shifts to the speaker's memorabilia from Camp 58, a logging camp located at the southern tip of McComas Meadows. The speaker explains their family's involvement with the camp, including their father's and uncles' work there, and shares anecdotes about the camp's cookhouse and the loggers' daily routines. The story of a woman named Ruth who planted a tree in front of the cookhouse, which still stands as a marker, illustrates the personal connections and lasting memories associated with the camp.

metadata.title: Connection to McComas Meadows and Camp 58
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the speaker's memorabilia from Camp 58, a logging camp located at the southern tip of McComas Meadows. The speaker explains their family's involvement with the camp, including their father's and uncles' work there, and shares anecdotes about the camp's cookhouse and the loggers' daily routines. The story of a woman named Ruth who planted a tree in front of the cookhouse, which still stands as a marker, illustrates the personal connections and lasting memories associated with the camp.

lines.line: # Camp 58 Details and Local Community

> The speaker provides a detailed account of Camp 58, describing its unique status as the only Potlatch camp in the region, its physical layout, and the bunkhouses and shops that once stood there. Most of the loggers were local, coming from nearby towns such as Grangeville, Kooskia, and Stites. The camp operated from 1941 to 1957, after which the bunkhouses were sold to local families, including the speaker's grandfather. The repurposing of these structures across the area reflects the camp's lasting impact on the community.

lines.line: # Outdoor Skills and Influence of Grandfather

> The speaker discusses the profound influence their grandfather had on their outdoor skills, including hunting, tracking, fishing, and shooting. They recount learning to track animals by observing subtle signs such as bruised leaves and impressions in the soil, skills that later aided them in law enforcement. The speaker's early experiences, such as shooting their first bear at eight years old, are highlighted as formative moments that shaped their abilities and interests.

metadata.title: Outdoor Skills and Influence of Grandfather

lines.line: # Law Enforcement Career and Notable Cases

> The speaker outlines their law enforcement career, beginning in Seattle in 1968 and continuing in Bellevue, Washington, where they served as a patrol officer and detective. They describe their work with the Drug Enforcement Administration Task Force, including undercover operations and encounters with organized crime figures. After returning to Idaho, the speaker joined the local police and sheriff's departments before becoming a private investigator, founding Gem State Investigation. Notable cases, such as the investigation into the murder of Forest Service officer Brett Jacobsen and the criminal activities of the Pratt brothers and Christopher Boyce, are recounted, illustrating the speaker's extensive experience and involvement in high-profile investigations.

metadata.title: Law Enforcement Career and Notable Cases
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines their law enforcement career, beginning in Seattle in 1968 and continuing in Bellevue, Washington, where they served as a patrol officer and detective. They describe their work with the Drug Enforcement Administration Task Force, including undercover operations and encounters with organized crime figures. After returning to Idaho, the speaker joined the local police and sheriff's departments before becoming a private investigator, founding Gem State Investigation. Notable cases, such as the investigation into the murder of Forest Service officer Brett Jacobsen and the criminal activities of the Pratt brothers and Christopher Boyce, are recounted, illustrating the speaker's extensive experience and involvement in high-profile investigations.

lines.line: # Return to Idaho and Running the Harpster Store

> After years in law enforcement and private investigation, the speaker decided to return to Idaho to raise their family away from urban challenges. They purchased the Harpster store and RV park with their wife Brenda, operating it for 23 years before retiring. The speaker reflects on the transition to retirement and Brenda's continued work, emphasizing their satisfaction with returning to a quieter, rural life.

metadata.title: Return to Idaho and Running the Harpster Store
metadata.synopsis: After years in law enforcement and private investigation, the speaker decided to return to Idaho to raise their family away from urban challenges. They purchased the Harpster store and RV park with their wife Brenda, operating it for 23 years before retiring. The speaker reflects on the transition to retirement and Brenda's continued work, emphasizing their satisfaction with returning to a quieter, rural life.

lines.line: # Near Miss with Airplane Crash

> The speaker recounts a harrowing experience involving a change in flight plans after a fishing trip in Mexico. Due to a rescheduled flight, they avoided boarding a plane that later crashed off the coast of Los Angeles, killing all passengers. The incident left a lasting impression, prompting reflection on fate and the realities of living in a remote area where travel logistics can have life-or-death consequences.

lines.line: # Childhood Memories and High School Experiences

> The speaker shares additional childhood memories, including a creative solution to free a fishing lure using a rifle, which impressed their grandfather. They also discuss their involvement in starting the first wrestling team at Grangeville High School in 1964, highlighting the camaraderie and pioneering spirit of their youth. These stories illustrate the speaker's problem-solving abilities and the formative experiences that shaped their character.

metadata.title: Childhood Memories and High School Experiences
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares additional childhood memories, including a creative solution to free a fishing lure using a rifle, which impressed their grandfather. They also discuss their involvement in starting the first wrestling team at Grangeville High School in 1964, highlighting the camaraderie and pioneering spirit of their youth. These stories illustrate the speaker's problem-solving abilities and the formative experiences that shaped their character.

lines.line: # Vietnam War Service

> The speaker describes their military service in the Navy during the Vietnam War, beginning with boot camp in San Diego and assignments in Hawaii and aboard the USS Herbert J. Thomas and USS Canberra. They recount exposure to hazardous substances during experimental operations, participation in shore bombardment during the Tet Offensive, and a close call with enemy fire. The narrative highlights the challenges and dangers faced during their service, as well as the prevalence of veterans in rural Idaho.

lines.line: # Reflections on the Good Old Days and Family

> The speaker reflects on the values and sense of community that characterized their childhood, recalling how neighbors came together to help build homes and support each other. They discuss their high school years, relationships, and the importance of family, including efforts to pass on outdoor skills to their grandchildren. The speaker notes changing attitudes toward hunting and the outdoors, observing that younger generations are less interested and that age has brought a greater appreciation for life.

children.text: # Early Life and Family Background

> The speaker describes their birth in Grangeville and upbringing in Harpster and McComas Meadows. They detail their family lineage, including their grandfather Lloyd Ruby and great uncle Jess McComas, and recount the family's settlement history in the area. The narrative includes the movement of the Shear family into Happy Hollow in 1877 and the speaker's childhood spent in Harpster until moving to Grangeville in 1954. The family's connection to the land, including the Ferris and Whitman homesteads, is emphasized, highlighting the generational ties and sense of home.
# Connection to McComas Meadows and Camp 58

> The conversation shifts to the speaker's memorabilia from Camp 58, a logging camp located at the southern tip of McComas Meadows. The speaker explains their family's involvement with the camp, including their father's and uncles' work there, and shares anecdotes about the camp's cookhouse and the loggers' daily routines. The story of a woman named Ruth who planted a tree in front of the cookhouse, which still stands as a marker, illustrates the personal connections and lasting memories associated with the camp.
# Camp 58 Details and Local Community

> The speaker provides a detailed account of Camp 58, describing its unique status as the only Potlatch camp in the region, its physical layout, and the bunkhouses and shops that once stood there. Most of the loggers were local, coming from nearby towns such as Grangeville, Kooskia, and Stites. The camp operated from 1941 to 1957, after which the bunkhouses were sold to local families, including the speaker's grandfather. The repurposing of these structures across the area reflects the camp's lasting impact on the community.
# Outdoor Skills and Influence of Grandfather

> The speaker discusses the profound influence their grandfather had on their outdoor skills, including hunting, tracking, fishing, and shooting. They recount learning to track animals by observing subtle signs such as bruised leaves and impressions in the soil, skills that later aided them in law enforcement. The speaker's early experiences, such as shooting their first bear at eight years old, are highlighted as formative moments that shaped their abilities and interests.
# Law Enforcement Career and Notable Cases

> The speaker outlines their law enforcement career, beginning in Seattle in 1968 and continuing in Bellevue, Washington, where they served as a patrol officer and detective. They describe their work with the Drug Enforcement Administration Task Force, including undercover operations and encounters with organized crime figures. After returning to Idaho, the speaker joined the local police and sheriff's departments before becoming a private investigator, founding Gem State Investigation. Notable cases, such as the investigation into the murder of Forest Service officer Brett Jacobsen and the criminal activities of the Pratt brothers and Christopher Boyce, are recounted, illustrating the speaker's extensive experience and involvement in high-profile investigations.
# Return to Idaho and Running the Harpster Store

> After years in law enforcement and private investigation, the speaker decided to return to Idaho to raise their family away from urban challenges. They purchased the Harpster store and RV park with their wife Brenda, operating it for 23 years before retiring. The speaker reflects on the transition to retirement and Brenda's continued work, emphasizing their satisfaction with returning to a quieter, rural life.
# Near Miss with Airplane Crash

> The speaker recounts a harrowing experience involving a change in flight plans after a fishing trip in Mexico. Due to a rescheduled flight, they avoided boarding a plane that later crashed off the coast of Los Angeles, killing all passengers. The incident left a lasting impression, prompting reflection on fate and the realities of living in a remote area where travel logistics can have life-or-death consequences.
# Childhood Memories and High School Experiences

> The speaker shares additional childhood memories, including a creative solution to free a fishing lure using a rifle, which impressed their grandfather. They also discuss their involvement in starting the first wrestling team at Grangeville High School in 1964, highlighting the camaraderie and pioneering spirit of their youth. These stories illustrate the speaker's problem-solving abilities and the formative experiences that shaped their character.
# Vietnam War Service

> The speaker describes their military service in the Navy during the Vietnam War, beginning with boot camp in San Diego and assignments in Hawaii and aboard the USS Herbert J. Thomas and USS Canberra. They recount exposure to hazardous substances during experimental operations, participation in shore bombardment during the Tet Offensive, and a close call with enemy fire. The narrative highlights the challenges and dangers faced during their service, as well as the prevalence of veterans in rural Idaho.
# Reflections on the Good Old Days and Family

> The speaker reflects on the values and sense of community that characterized their childhood, recalling how neighbors came together to help build homes and support each other. They discuss their high school years, relationships, and the importance of family, including efforts to pass on outdoor skills to their grandchildren. The speaker notes changing attitudes toward hunting and the outdoors, observing that younger generations are less interested and that age has brought a greater appreciation for life.
# Final Thoughts and Gratitude

> The speaker concludes by acknowledging the mistakes made throughout life but expresses deep gratitude for being from the area. They emphasize the treasure of their home and the importance of returning to their roots, ending the interview on a note of appreciation and contentment.

story.title: Monty Spears -03/18/26
story.description: The interview provides an oral history of a man reflecting on his life, including his upbringing in Harpster and Grangeville, Idaho, and the profound influence of his family, particularly his grandfather. Noteworthy topics include his military service in the Navy during the Vietnam War, experiences in law enforcement—where he served in Seattle and was later involved with the Drug Enforcement Administration—and his career as a private investigator. The discussion also touches on local history, such as Camp 58, family heritage, and generational changes in values, especially concerning outdoor activities. The narrative highlights a sense of nostalgia for community connections and the natural beauty of the area.
